past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 8 years ago, on oral hypoglycemics
Coronary artery disease with prior percutaneous coronary intervention to LAD 4 years ago
Mild chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline creatinine 1.3 mg/dL)

medications.indication: Hypertension and rate control for atrial fibrillation

family_history: Father died of myocardial infarction at age 62
Mother had hypertension and type 2 diabetes, died at age 78 of congestive heart failure
Brother has a pacemaker for sick sinus syndrome, placed at age 68

social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 30-pack-year history, quit 10 years ago
social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine with dinner, 2-3 times per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a single-story home
social_history.exercise: Sedentary, limited by fatigue and mild exertional dyspnea over past several months

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech is fluent with normal rate and prosody. No dysarthria. Attention intact, able to spell WORLD backwards. Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No confusion or post-ictal features at time of examination.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam unremarkable, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch in all three divisions bilaterally. Masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag reflex intact. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline, no fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal in all extremities, no rigidity or spasticity. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups tested bilaterally (deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip, hip flexors, knee extensors, ankle dorsiflexors, plantarflexors). No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, proprioception, and vibration intact throughout. Mildly diminished vibration at bilateral great toes, consistent with mild peripheral neuropathy.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor (downgoing) bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing normal bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing normal bilaterally. No intention tremor. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady, normal base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. No ataxia.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration or oral trauma. No signs of urinary incontinence. No bruising to suggest prolonged convulsive activity. Small laceration on right forehead from fall, hemostasis achieved.

patient.chief_complaint: Found on the floor after a witnessed sudden collapse at home
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Gerald Whitfield is a 70-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes who was brought to the emergency department by EMS after his wife witnessed him suddenly collapse at home. The patient was seated at the kitchen table eating breakfast at approximately 8:15 AM when he suddenly slumped forward and fell to the floor without any preceding warning. His wife, who was standing nearby, reports that there was no cry out, no tonic posturing, and no rhythmic jerking of the extremities. She states he appeared pale and 'looked gray' during the event. He was unresponsive for approximately 20-30 seconds before regaining consciousness. Upon awakening, he was immediately alert and oriented, asking what had happened. There was no period of confusion, no tongue biting, and no urinary incontinence. He sustained a small forehead laceration from striking the kitchen floor. The patient reports that over the past three weeks he has experienced two prior episodes of 'nearly blacking out' characterized by sudden lightheadedness and a sensation that the room was going dark, each lasting only a few seconds. He did not lose consciousness during those episodes but had to sit down. He has also noticed increasing fatigue and mild exertional dyspnea when walking to the mailbox over the past two months. He denies chest pain, palpitations preceding the event, or recent illness. He reports a past episode of palpitations several months ago but was told his atrial fibrillation was 'controlled.' His wife confirms that he has never had a seizure before and that this episode looked distinctly different from seizures she witnessed her late sister experience.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-regulated, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Brief drowsiness captured with vertex waves, no sleep architecture abnormalities

artifacts.description: Intermittent, does not obscure underlying activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. No significant change in background. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. No epileptiform abnormalities identified. No seizures captured during this 40-minute routine recording. This study does not support a diagnosis of epilepsy. Clinical correlation is recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 40-minute recording; interictal EEG sensitivity for epilepsy is limited to approximately 50% on a single study.
recommended_actions: If clinical suspicion for seizure remains, consider prolonged or ambulatory EEG monitoring
Correlate with cardiac monitoring given the clinical presentation

ecg.rhythm: Complete heart block (third-degree AV block) with ventricular escape rhythm
findings: Complete AV dissociation with independent atrial rate of approximately 78 bpm
Ventricular escape rhythm at 35 bpm with wide QRS morphology
QRS morphology suggestive of right bundle branch block pattern in escape beats
Left axis deviation consistent with left anterior fascicular block
ST depression in leads V4-V6 (likely rate-related or ischemic)
No acute ST elevation to suggest STEMI
ecg.interpretation: Third-degree (complete) atrioventricular block with slow ventricular escape rhythm at 35 beats per minute. AV dissociation confirmed by independent P waves marching through QRS complexes. Widened QRS with RBBB morphology in escape rhythm. Left axis deviation. Borderline prolonged QTc. This is a high-risk ECG finding that explains the syncopal event and requires urgent cardiology consultation and likely temporary transcutaneous or transvenous pacing with subsequent permanent pacemaker implantation.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Complete heart block with slow ventricular escape rhythm is the most likely etiology of this patient's syncope. The low ventricular rate leads to decreased cardiac output and cerebral hypoperfusion, resulting in transient loss of consciousness. This is consistent with a Stokes-Adams attack.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mildly low, likely related to chronic kidney disease

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, consistent with known CKD stage 3a
Mildly elevated, near baseline for patient with CKD 3a
Elevated, consistent with known diabetes mellitus, non-fasting specimen

Cardiac_markers.clinical_significance: At upper limit of normal, likely demand-related in setting of bradycardia; serial monitoring recommended
Elevated, suggests underlying cardiac dysfunction, may be related to chronic bradycardia and reduced cardiac output

Coagulation.clinical_significance: Patient on apixaban, which does not significantly affect INR

Metabolic.clinical_significance: Normal thyroid function, hypothyroidism ruled out as contributor to bradycardia

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results are largely unremarkable with no acute metabolic derangement to explain syncope. Electrolytes are normal, ruling out hypokalemia or hyperkalemia as contributors to conduction abnormality. BUN and creatinine are mildly elevated at baseline consistent with known CKD. Troponin is at the upper limit of normal, likely demand-related in setting of severe bradycardia; serial troponins recommended to exclude acute coronary syndrome. BNP elevation suggests chronic cardiac dysfunction. Glucose is elevated in the setting of known diabetes. TSH is normal.

Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Mildly reduced systolic function, likely related to chronic bradycardia and prior coronary artery disease
Mildly dilated, consistent with history of atrial fibrillation
Mildly increased, consistent with hypertensive heart disease
Age-related degenerative changes; may contribute to calcific infiltration of the conduction system
Mild, functional, likely secondary to LV dysfunction

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates mildly reduced left ventricular systolic function (LVEF 45%) with mild left atrial dilation. Mild interventricular septal hypertrophy consistent with hypertensive heart disease. Aortic valve sclerosis without hemodynamically significant stenosis is noted. Mild mitral regurgitation. No pericardial effusion. No intracardiac masses or thrombus identified. The aortic valve sclerosis raises the possibility that degenerative calcification may be extending into the conduction system, contributing to the patient's complete heart block.

output.rhythm: Persistent complete heart block with ventricular escape rhythm throughout 24-hour recording
findings: Persistent complete AV block throughout 24-hour recording
Atrial rate averaging 76 bpm with intermittent atrial fibrillation (approximately 15% of recording)
Minimum ventricular rate 28 bpm at 03:42 AM with 4.2-second RR pause
Maximum ventricular rate 42 bpm during brief ambulation
Rare isolated PVCs (32 total in 24 hours)
output.interpretation: 24-hour Holter monitor confirms persistent complete (third-degree) AV block with slow, unreliable ventricular escape rhythm. Critically slow rates overnight with pauses up to 4.2 seconds. Intermittent atrial fibrillation noted but underlying complete AV block is persistent. This confirms a high-risk conduction disturbance requiring permanent pacemaker implantation.
output.clinical_correlation: The 4.2-second pause and persistent complete heart block confirm a high-risk substrate for recurrent syncope and potential sudden cardiac death. Urgent permanent pacemaker implantation is strongly indicated. A dual-chamber pacemaker (DDD) or cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) should be considered given reduced LVEF.

Tilt_table_test.clinical_significance: Orthostatic drop, likely secondary to inability to augment cardiac output due to fixed ventricular rate
No chronotropic response due to complete heart block
Symptoms reproduced due to hemodynamic compromise from fixed low heart rate
No cardioinhibitory or vasodepressor vasovagal response identified; orthostatic intolerance is secondary to bradycardia

output.interpretation: Tilt table test does not demonstrate a vasovagal mechanism. The orthostatic blood pressure drop and symptom reproduction are attributable to the patient's underlying complete heart block with fixed low ventricular rate, preventing appropriate cardiac output augmentation with upright posture. Vasovagal syncope is effectively ruled out. The primary etiology remains cardiac conduction disease.
abnormal_values_summary: Orthostatic BP drop during tilt (94/56 from 112/64)

output.rhythm: Persistent complete heart block; ventricular escape rhythm did not appropriately increase with exercise
findings: Test terminated early at Stage 1 of Bruce protocol (1.7 mph, 10% grade) after 2 minutes 45 seconds due to symptomatic hypotension and presyncope
Peak ventricular rate 40 bpm (severely blunted chronotropic response due to complete heart block)
Atrial rate increased to 112 bpm but with persistent AV dissociation
Blood pressure fell from 108/62 to 88/50 at peak exercise
1 mm ST depression in leads V5-V6 at peak exercise (demand ischemia in setting of severe bradycardia)
output.interpretation: Exercise stress test limited by symptomatic hypotension and presyncope in setting of complete heart block. Chronotropic incompetence due to complete AV block. Mild ST depression at peak exercise suggestive of demand ischemia secondary to severe bradycardia rather than primary coronary lesion. Test confirms the hemodynamic significance of the complete heart block and the need for pacing.
output.clinical_correlation: The inability to augment heart rate with exercise confirms chronotropic incompetence from complete heart block. This test reinforces the indication for permanent pacemaker implantation. Rate-responsive pacing should be considered.

signal_characteristics.T1: Mildly elevated native T1 values in the basal septum
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Mid-wall linear enhancement in basal interventricular septum consistent with non-ischemic fibrosis pattern
Thin subendocardial enhancement consistent with prior infarction

additional_observations: No evidence of arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy
No evidence of cardiac sarcoidosis or amyloidosis
output.impression: Cardiac MRI demonstrates mid-wall fibrosis in the basal interventricular septum, which is in the region of the bundle of His and proximal bundle branches. This non-ischemic fibrosis pattern is consistent with idiopathic degenerative fibrosis of the conduction system (Lenegre-Lev disease) and provides a structural substrate for the patient's complete heart block. Additionally, there is evidence of a small prior myocardial infarction in the mid-anterior wall with subendocardial enhancement. LVEF is mildly reduced at 45%. No features of ARVC, sarcoidosis, or infiltrative cardiomyopathy.
recommended_actions: Permanent pacemaker implantation for symptomatic complete heart block
Consider CRT-P given LVEF 45% and anticipated high ventricular pacing burden
Continued medical therapy for coronary artery disease

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Mid-wall septal fibrosis at the level of the conduction system with complete heart block in an elderly patient with aortic valve sclerosis
Prior anterior MI may have partially contributed to conduction system damage

CT_Head_Report.clinical_significance: Age-related chronic small vessel ischemic disease, not clinically significant

CT_Head_Report.value: Small right frontal subgaleal soft tissue swelling
CT_Head_Report.clinical_significance: Consistent with reported fall and forehead laceration, no underlying fracture

output.interpretation: Non-contrast CT head is unremarkable for acute intracranial pathology. No intracranial hemorrhage, mass, or acute ischemic changes. Mild chronic microvascular disease appropriate for age. Small right frontal soft tissue swelling consistent with the reported fall. No fracture. This effectively rules out an intracranial cause for the patient's syncopal episode.
abnormal_values_summary: Right frontal subgaleal soft tissue swelling (post-traumatic, from fall)

additional_observations: No acute infarction on DWI/ADC sequences
output.impression: Brain MRI is unremarkable for acute pathology. Mild chronic small vessel ischemic disease appropriate for patient's age and cardiovascular risk factors. No acute infarction, hemorrhage, mass, or demyelinating lesion. Brain MRI does not reveal a neurological cause for the patient's syncopal episode.
recommended_actions: Focus evaluation on cardiac etiology for syncope

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to complete (third-degree) atrioventricular block

differential.key_distinguishing: No tonic-clonic activity witnessed, no post-ictal confusion, no tongue bite, no urinary incontinence, normal EEG. Wife specifically noted this looked different from seizures.
No identifiable trigger (pain, prolonged standing, emotional stress), no prodromal symptoms of nausea or diaphoresis, age and cardiac history favor cardiac etiology.
Event occurred while seated (not on standing), on antihypertensives and beta-blocker but no positional component. Any orthostatic changes are secondary to fixed low heart rate.
No focal neurological deficits, TIA rarely causes true loss of consciousness, normal neurological exam, normal brain imaging.
No dyspnea, chest pain, tachycardia, or hypoxia. SpO2 96%. No risk factors for DVT. Syncope mechanism is clearly explained by complete heart block.

optimal_actions.expected_finding: Complete heart block with ventricular escape rhythm at 35 bpm

optimal_actions.action: Continuous telemetry monitoring and establish IV access
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Persistent bradycardia with complete AV dissociation

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, troponin, BNP, coagulation studies, TSH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No metabolic cause for syncope, troponin borderline, BNP elevated

optimal_actions.action: Prepare for transcutaneous pacing and have atropine available for symptomatic bradycardia
Urgent cardiology consultation for temporary pacing and permanent pacemaker planning
Obtain EEG to evaluate for epileptiform abnormalities and rule out seizure
Obtain transthoracic echocardiogram to assess structural heart disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mildly reduced LVEF, aortic sclerosis, LA dilation

optimal_actions.action: Hold metoprolol and other AV-nodal blocking agents
Admit to telemetry unit or CCU for monitoring pending pacemaker implantation
Plan for permanent pacemaker implantation (dual-chamber DDD or CRT-P given reduced LVEF)

critical_actions: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately to identify complete heart block
Hold metoprolol and all AV-nodal blocking agents immediately
Have transcutaneous pacing pads applied and ready
Recognize that the syncope is cardiac in origin and not a seizure
contraindicated_actions: Initiating antiepileptic drugs based on syncope misdiagnosed as seizure
Administering beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers that further suppress AV conduction
Discharging the patient without cardiac monitoring and pacemaker evaluation
Performing lumbar puncture (no indication, patient is on anticoagulation)
Administering IV adenosine (would worsen AV block)
Ordering carotid Doppler as primary evaluation (syncope is not TIA)
key_reasoning_points: The sudden, unheralded loss of consciousness without warning or prodrome is characteristic of cardiac syncope (Stokes-Adams attack) rather than vasovagal syncope or seizure
The absence of tonic-clonic activity, post-ictal confusion, tongue biting, and urinary incontinence argues strongly against an epileptic seizure
Rapid return to full alertness after the event is typical of cardiac syncope and atypical for seizure (which usually has a post-ictal period)
The ECG showing complete heart block with ventricular escape at 35 bpm provides the definitive diagnosis and explains the syncopal mechanism
The patient's history of coronary artery disease, atrial fibrillation, and aortic sclerosis suggests degenerative conduction system disease (Lenegre-Lev disease) as the underlying etiology
Metoprolol (a beta-blocker) may be contributing to the severity of the bradycardia and must be held immediately
The normal EEG supports the cardiac rather than epileptic etiology of the transient loss of consciousness
Elevated BNP suggests chronic cardiac dysfunction from sustained bradycardia, further supporting the need for permanent pacing

metadata.author: NeuroBench generation pipeline
clinical_pearls: Convulsive syncope (brief myoclonic jerks from cerebral hypoperfusion) can be mistaken for epileptic seizures
Cardiac syncope typically has no prodrome and rapid recovery without post-ictal confusion
Complete heart block is a Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation regardless of symptoms
Lenegre-Lev disease (idiopathic degenerative fibrosis of the conduction system) is the most common cause of acquired complete heart block in the elderly
